3	Stefan Georgi
BONUS 3	Video 1 - Big Ideas Explained
Big Ideas Explained
VIDEO 1
Big Ideas Explained
(00:01)
All right. So in this module we are going to go over the big idea, which is honestly
one of the most confusing and tricky things for people. Тo be completely
honest with you, I don’t spend a ton of time consciously thinking about the big
idea, but I do tend to have big ideas in what I’m doing and I think it’s more of
a subconscious process for me. But that being said, obviously, that doesn’t
really help you so I do have a training. This is based on something I did for my
Copy Accelerator members a few months back for our mastermind but then
I’ve expanded upon it and spent some time working on it. With that being said,
let’s just jump right in and we’re going to look at quite a bit of things, actually.
This is 15 pages, but hopefully, it’ll help a lot.
(01:05)
So first of all, you need to define the big idea, but even that isn’t actually really
easy. David Ogilvy, who’s obviously one of the masters of marketing and great
advertisers of all time, he defines the big idea this way. He says, “You can do
homework from now until doomsday, but you will never win fame and fortune
unless you also invent big ideas. It takes a big idea to attract the attention of
consumers and get them to buy your product. Research can’t help you much
because it cannot predict the cumulative value of an idea and no idea is big
unless it will work for 30 years.”
(01:41)
Okay, David. I feel like the grandioseness with which marketers, advertisers
talked from back in the 50s or I don’t know when the fuck Ogilvy was alive,
honestly. All right, cool. I think the part here is, “It takes a big idea to attract the
attention of customers and get them to buy the product.” Kind of, but he’s also
talking about the unique selling proposition and we’re going to differentiate
between the two a little bit further down.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 17 --

4	Stefan Georgi
BONUS 3	Video 1 - Big Ideas Explained
(02:12)
But here’s Ogilvy’s checklist, which I do think generally is good at least parts
of it. Did it make me gasp when I first saw it? Do I wish I had thought of it
myself? Is it unique? Does it fit the strategy to perfection? Could it be used for
30 years? I like that. Then here’s really the best version of it from Ogilvy, “A
big idea is an idea that is instantly comprehended as important, exciting, and
beneficial. It also leads to an inevitable conclusion, a conclusion that makes
it easy to sell your product.” Actually, this is the best definition of a big idea
I’ve ever seen. I think this is it. We’ll come back to this idea - well, Ogilvy’s part
of it in this training.
(02:59)
What John Forde says and I think this is taken - let me see something really quick
here. Just give me one second, I’ll make sure I give the right attribution. There’s
John Forde, but then there’s also this thing from Mark Ford, I believe. I wanted
to make sure I was giving the right attribution because what I’m getting this
next part from is from Craig Ballantyne’s blog and Craig Ballantyne’s website
actually, which was written by Mark Ford, but he’s talking about something that
John Forde told him so I wanted to make sure I wasn’t mixing it.
(03:30)
What John Forde says, at least in this article, “Think about it. When you have
a great conversation, read a great book, or see a great documentary, what
grabs you? Is it the litany of small details, or the golden thread that unites
them? More often than not, for most of us it’s the ladder. And the more you
get the core idea behind a story, a speech or revelation, the more memorable
that one core message becomes. This is just as true in the sales copy. One
message well-developed just has more impact than ads, short or long, that
are overloaded with competing ideas.”
(04:02)
That’s actually - I mean it’s really, really, really good, too because again, going
back to what did Ogilvy say, “A big idea is an idea that is instantly comprehended
as important, exciting and beneficial. It also leads to an inevitable conclusion,
a conclusion that makes it easy to sell your product.” What you have here with
John Forde is basically the point of being you grasp something intuitively and
it’s something that you understand and also, that’s shareable as you’re going
to see. So there are two resources that I have there, one is, like I mentioned,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 17 --

5	Stefan Georgi
BONUS 3	Video 1 - Big Ideas Explained
from Craig’s website and it’s written by Mark Ford and then he references
John Forde in it. But it’s a good thing about big ideas so worth reading that
and then from AWAI, the American Writers and Artists Institute, they have a
checklist for big ideas and they talk about Ogilvy and everything; it’s the next
resource as well.
(05:06)
All right. For my my criteria for big ideas in sales copy, I think if you look at your
writing, the questions I have is one, “will I be able to quickly and succinctly
explain the big idea behind it to my friends or family?” And two, “will I be able
to do it without having to go back to the promo for any reminders?” That’s
super important. We want something that it’s almost like a meme; one way it
may help you to think about your big idea and your sales copy.
(05:38)
The technical definition of a meme is “an idea, behavior, style or usage that
spreads from person to person within a culture,” but basically great, whatever
on that. “But in reality, most memes that go viral are ones that are short, and
immediately, intellectually accessible and that elicit an emotional response
whether that’s humor, sometimes just anger, or curiosity, et cetera. I just found
a couple of silly memes. “Ate spaghetti while wearing a white shirt, didn’t get
sauce on it.” I don’t have to go back to remember what this meme is, I’m like
oh, that’s funny because every time I wear a wear a white shirt, I get a stain
on it. This one, you’re intuitively are like yeah and then you would share it with
others because you’re like yeah, I get this. I bet they’ll get it if I share it with
them, too.
(06:23)
This one I just found this morning, I think it’s funny. “When Twilight is over
and so is your career.” It’s just funny, the actor guy having put on weight is
funny, but then the picture of the wolf being fat after that just cracks me up.
So it makes me laugh, it elicits an emotional response and something I could
quickly and easily describe to somebody even if I didn’t share the meme. I’d
be like oh, it’s funny. It shows the actor from Twilight when he was in the
movies and then now he’s put on weight, but then it shows the wolf, too and
the wolf looks all majestic, but then it shows the wolf now and he’s fat and
out of shape. It won’t be as funny as seeing it, but at least I can explain it to
somebody and they would get it right away.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Then this one. I don’t have even a text for this one, but you’ve seen this for
a million different things, this meme, of the wandering eyes boyfriend or

checking her out, she’s walking by maybe oblivious, but maybe not because
she’s smiling. That picture is just a great picture for it.
(07:52)
Another way to look at big ideas and to understand them is to look at nonfiction
because again, remember really just looking for that one instantly graspable,
instantly understandable, easy to grasp the idea that you get and that you
intuitively could share with others and then to Ogilvy’s point, that makes it
easy and logical to buy the product when you get to that part, too. I think
business books do a good job of this, the most successful ones. I have some
lists here.
(08:30)
One is Start With Why by Simon Sinek, which is a great book and the big idea
is basically that “We spend all the time talking about the “what” and “how”of
things. But the most effective people in organizations first identify their “why.”
Their “why” is the big driver behind everything they do. Getting clear on your
“why” can guide everything you do.” If I was trying to tell somebody about
the book, I’d be like oh, have you read Start With Why by Simon Sinek? and
someone might be like, “No, I haven’t.” Basically, it’s about how we spend
all this time asking how we’re going to do things, or what we’re going to do,
things like that but we don’t really ask why we’re doing those things. This
is true for people, but also for businesses and organizations. But the most
successful organizations actually start with why and that’s the whole big idea
behind the book and it also makes it easy to want to get the book because if
you hear that and you’re curious, that resonates with you then you’re like, “Oh,
I should read that book.”
(09:27)
From Good to Great by Jim Collins. It’s another classic business book. The big idea
is that “There are certain predictable factors that enable mediocre companies to
become great companies. When organizations apply these factors, they too can
make the leap.” Pretty easy, pretty obvious. Basically, in From Good to Great, Jim
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

7	Stefan Georgi
BONUS 3	Video 1 - Big Ideas Explained
Collins says that there’s a predictable checklist of things that great companies
do and that mediocre companies do those that follow that checklist, they can
become great as well. Pretty easy. We’ll do two more.
(10:03)
The Seven Habits of Highly Effective People by Stephen Covey. Classic book
for personal development. The big idea, “There are seven habits that the most
effective people strictly adhere to, and the rest of us don’t. By tackling these
habits in sequential order, we can become the most powerful and effective
version of ourselves.” Really, the big idea is the title of the book. There’s seven
habits that highly effective people have, that the rest of us don’t have and
that’s really just about it and then I would add in maybe the sequential part
because that is important to the book, but maybe that’s even not as important
as just the fact that there are seven habits of highly effective people.
(10:41)
The last one would be The One Thing by Gary Keller, which is another great boo
and here it’s basically that “The key to success is, paradoxically, to do less.
The key to success and effectiveness in both the personal and professional
spheres is to cut out the BS and focus on the single largest needle mover.”
Again, it’d be like oh, have you read The One Thing? Basically, it’s that you
should just focus on doing less and just focus on your one biggest needle
mover and let everything else fall and that paradoxically, when you do less
you actually accomplish more. It’s the big idea of that book; easy to explain or
describe to other people.
(11:19)
Then I always go back to the Pimsleur approach, Pimsleur method, which
is a sales letter that Chris Haddad wrote for Dan Roitman and those guys.
Basically, the big idea for that promotion and for the product is that “It’s easy -
seemingly effortless - for a child to learn a new language while it’s exceedingly
hard for adults. The reason why is because children learn innately, while adults
are taught through rote learning, memorization, and drilling. So if you want to
learn new language, you should learn like a child does.” I think I copy/pasted
this directly from the sales letter, but pretty intuitive. It’s really easy for kids
to learn languages, but the reason why I say learn differently - actually, it’s
really easy for a kid to learn a new language, but it’s really hard for an adult to
learn a new language. Why? Children learn in this innate fashion while adults
learn through memorization and this academic version of it, but that doesn’t
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 17 --

8	Stefan Georgi
BONUS 3	Video 1 - Big Ideas Explained
work as well. So if we want to learn a language, we should try to learn it in the
way that a child does. That’s just one that’s always really resonated with me
because it’s very intuitive to understand and if you understand it, then it’s very
easy to be like oh, okay. If this product teaches me to learn the language in the
way that a child does, I should buy it if you’re the target market.
(12:41)
The way I approach this, a lot of times it’s mechanism-driven. Obviously, you
have to do your research still. You have to know who the market is and what
their pain point is at least and the pain point they want solved and then actually,
you need to look at the mechanism too. What’s the traditional explanation of
the problem? What’s their traditional explanation of the solution? Is there a new
contrarian explanation of the problem or solution that catches your attention?
Do any of those new or contrarian, explanations make you excited? Do you
believe that based off your research, your target market will also feel excited?
Can you find ample evidence to support these newer contrarian explanations?
You’ll see, we’ll look at examples of this here.
(13:37)
Sometimes the easiest and best place to find that information is by simply
searching news stories about the subject. For example, I did a product on
telomeres. Telomeres are basically the links - they’re the end caps of your
DNA so your DNA has a strand and there’s these two caps on the end and
there’s all of this research that says that telomeres are related to longevity,
living longer and health, not getting sick and all this stuff. I’m going to open up
a ton of these examples here. I had these books open, but we don’t even need
to really look at them. Let’s open up a bunch of these.
(14:27)
When I was doing research for this telomere product, I found this one, “New
doctors’ DNA ages six times faster than normal in the first year.” “Long work
hours of intern year associative with accelerated shortening of telomere
regions of chromosomes. That’s pretty interesting. Do doctors’ DNA age six
times faster than than the normal in a year? Basically, if you read the reason
why, it’s because of the stress and long hours that that undermines their DNA.
So the big idea for this one could be that stress and lack of sleep age your
DNA and specifically, the telomeres can keep you young.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

(15:16)
This one, “Telomeres are the new cholesterol, now what?” “Parts of our
chromosomes called telomeres are seen as sort of aging biomarker. Long,
you’ve got a while. Short, don’t buy any green bananas. But what do we do with
that information?” which even that’s just saying the big idea of telomeres is
that when you have long telomeres, you live a long time; when you have short
telomeres, you don’t live as long. So that could be a big idea of a promo.
(15:53)
The thing is there’s also the market awareness and sophistication; that’s why
if we go back to what I said up here about the new contrarian explanation of
the problem or solution is important and why we need to do the research for
the market, too because the thing is if you research, a lot of the market actually
for an anti-aging or a telomere product is already familiar with telomeres. So
the big idea of telomeres is great; we can use that to explain in the sales
copy, but it wouldn’t be the big idea of the actual promo because it’s not
unique enough. Does that make sense? But the idea of “Telomeres are the
new cholesterol” is interesting and if you read this article, it’s basically going
to talk about how rather than using cholesterol as a marker for your health,
measuring your telomeres would be a better option. Got it, so that was that.
(16:47)
Remember, the first one, stress and lack of sleep make your DNA and telomeres
age faster. This one’s that telomeres are the new cholesterol; looking at the
length of your telomeres is an indicator of how healthy you are.

“Oxidative stress acts directly on telomeres to hasten cellular aging.” This
one is basically oxidative stress, which comes about from pollution, smoking,
obesity all shorten your telomeres and obviously, stress is essentially tie into
inflammation and things like that too.
(17:27)
This one’s pretty interesting, “NASA’s landmark twins study reveals the

NASA astronaut Scott Kelly and his twin brother participated in an investigation
and found that - Basically, Scott Kelly spent a bunch of time in space and his
brother didn’t and they wanted to see how their DNA was affected. Basically,
what they found is that for Scott, the guy who was in space, his telomeres

were longer in space but then they became shorter when he returned to Earth

This one is actually a similar article about this NASA twin study, but you can

(18:50)
There’s this one, “Rate of telomere shortening predicts species lifespan.”
I’ll make this font a little bigger. “A flamingo lives 40 years and a human
being lives 90 years, a mouse lives 2 years and an elephant lives 60. Why?
What determines the lifespan of a species? After analyzing nine species of
mammals and birds, researchers at the Spanish National Cancer Research
Center found a very clear relationship between the lifespan of these species
and the shortening rate of their telomeres, the structures that protect the
chromosomes and the genes they contain. The relationship is expressed as
a mathematical equation, a formula that can accurately predict the longevity
of the species. The study was done in collaboration with the Madrid Zoo
Aquarium and the University of Barcelona.
(19:29)
“The telomere shortening rate is a powerful predictor of species lifespan,” the
authors write in the prestigious journal Proceedings of the National Academy
of Sciences (PNAS).
(19:39)
The study compares the telomeres of mice, goats, dolphins, gulls, reindeer,
vultures, flamingos, elephants and humans and reveals that species whose
telomeres shorten faster have shorter lives.
(19:51)
The relationship can be fitted to a certain type of mathematical curve - a power
law curve, which the authors explained in PNAS.” Oh, sorry, makes sense
here. “A power law curve, which the authors explained in PNAS, is also used
to describe other processes such as population growth, city size, species

extinction, body mass, individual income.

To Maria Blasco, Head of the Telomeres and Telomerase Group of the CNIO

(20:24)
What counts is not their length, but the rate at which they shorten. In the case

(20:30)
Let me see if I want to keep going here. I’ll just skip to this paragraph.
(20:38)
“It has been known for quite some time, thanks in large part to the work of
Blasco’s group, that telomeres are at the basis of aging of the organism.
Telomeres make up the ends of chromosomes inside the cell nucleus; their
function is to protect the genes. However, each time the cells multiply to repair
damage, their telomeres become a little shorter. Throughout life, it may happen
that telomeres shorten too much and cannot regenerate anymore. When that
happens, the cell stops functioning normally.
(21:01)
Until now, however, no relationship had been found between telomere length
and lifespan of each species. There are species with very long telomeres that
are short-lived and vice versa.
(21:09)
The CNIO researchers decided not to compare the absolute length of the
telomeres, but rather their rate of shortening. It is the first large-scale study that
compares this highly variable parameter between species. Human telomeres
lose on average about 7,000 base pairs - the building blocks of the genetic
material - per year, whereas those of mice lose about 7,000 base pairs per year.
(21:31)
To Kurt Whittemore, first author of the paper, the study confirms that telomeres

play an important role in aging. There are people who are not convinced, and

Okay, cool. Let’s come back to this one, but just remember - going back to this

you can’t turn it off,” said senior author Bennett Van Houten, Ph.D., professor

of pharmacology and chemical biology at the University of Pittsburgh School

So 	this 	one 	is 	super 	interesting 	to 	me, 	because 	this 	idea 	of 	runaway
mitochondria causing telomere damage, the idea of cellular pollution or the
Chernobyl effect; those are really interesting ideas. Now that being said, having
gone through all of these, which one did I choose? Ultimately, I chose this one.
This is for a promo I actually did. Why this one and not the Chernobyl effect or
doctors aging, or anything like that? I liked the contrarian aspects. Going back
to my... I liked the contrarian aspect of it. Remember, traditional explanation
of the problem, solution, contrarian, target market will be interested.
(25:07)
So what I liked about this one is a couple of things. It was really easy to
understand; it’s not the length of your telomeres, it’s the rate at which they
shrink. I had this idea of: Your shrinking telomeres are responsible for aging,
getting old, all of that. That’s very easy. The reason you’re getting old is
because you have telomeres in your body and they’re shrinking and the faster
they shrink, the faster you age. It’s very intuitive. I also like the animal stuff
because this whole article just starts with a paradoxical question. I think that’s
really interesting. There’s a testable proof thing about why mice don’t live as
long as humans, even though they have more telomeres than us at the start.
There’s the whole idea of a mathematical equation, which was very interesting
to me. All that, so that’s why.
(26:02)
I think this one, the “Runaway mitochondria causing telomere damage” is very
interesting. But mitochondria, if I pick that I’d have to then explain mitochondria
and that’s going to bore people, take time and all that. So I think it’s saying
that’s interesting, but not... It’s more challenging than this idea of I already to
have to explain telomeres in the promo, but now I have to explain telomeres
and mitochondria and blah, blah, blah. But with this one, I’m just talking about
how everyone’s got telomeres, they’re important, they’re the end caps on DNA,
it doesn’t matter how many you have or how long they are at the beginning, but
what matters is how fast your telomeres shrink and again, there’s a direct link
between the speed with which your telomeres shrink and the speed at which
you get old, sick, and all of that. Really intuitive and easy to grasp.

Started this whole thing with, “Lots of people know about telomeres, and they’ve
heard that shrinking telomeres are a hallmark of aging, disease and death.

have longer telomeres than humans. Yet they only live 2 years. Humans live
78 years. Why? Because the telomeres in mice are programmed to shrink at a
much quicker rate than humans do and they die much faster as a result.

Spanish researchers looked at nine different animals, including humans, and

Well, in my opinion, if someone’s interested in anti-aging and telomeres and

cause you to age, the contrarian aspect it’s not the length of your telomeres,

(29:46)
All right, let’s look at some FAQs here. What role does the story play? In my
opinion, the story is used to illustrate the big idea, but the story is not itself the
big idea. I think it’s important to have a really good story and that’s curiosity,
emotionally driven and all of that. Generally, having a story is important, but
it’s not the actual big idea. Is the headline the big idea? I think that the headline
will often promote the big idea, but sometimes it doesn’t. I think in Pimsleur
it didn’t, but I’m not going to share the Pimsleur transcript in this course
because it’s not mine. I want to be respectful. “But it’s not the big idea.” Is the
mechanism the big idea? I think it’s close, but the mechanism is lengthier and
the big idea is ultimately, the single idea behind your argument or promise
that someone can quickly understand and easily share with others.
(31:30)
A couple of examples here. Burn, which you may remember from the copy
module. I’ll pull it up here. “Harvard scientists discovered biological loophole
that forces your body to burn all of your stored fat at once.” Really, that’s
the big idea and it’s in the headline, too. It’s like, “Harvard has discovered a
loophole that lets your body burn all of your stored fat at the same time. It’s
called “simultaneous fat release.”” The idea with Burn is instead of burning fat
in just your belly or hips or one place, there’s a way to trigger your body so that
you can burn fat everywhere at the same time. That’s the big idea of burn fat
everywhere. Hence, the name Burn.
(32:21)
How about biz-opp? We looked at that biz opp example about the closeout
stuff, closeout sales. Well, really, the big idea there was that you can make
money acting as the middleman between retail stores and liquidators, and
you don’t need any formal education or training to do it. That’s the big idea
and that’s basically it. There’s also the USP, I guess in this case so maybe
sometimes they blend together, but that to me, is the big idea behind the
promo. That’s the big idea that can be instantly transferable to others. If you
read that promo, you’re like, “Oh, there’s a ton of retail stores that aren’t able
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 17 --

16	Stefan Georgi
BONUS 3	Video 1 - Big Ideas Explained
to sell all their stuff and there’s a ton of liquidators who want to buy that stuff
from those retail stores. So if you can act as a middleman, you can make a
commission.” It’s easy to understand and explain to somebody else.
(33:21)
That’s the big idea at least the way I teach it, the way I look at it. I think people
almost maybe put too much stock into the big idea. I think it’s very important
but again, I don’t know, I take that back. I just do it intuitively, but I understand
that maybe some people don’t so if that’s you and you don’t intuitively do
it, that’s fine, of course. But now hopefully, with this training, it makes more
sense and you can work on developing a big idea. The best test for it again, is
to just be able to go talk to somebody, tell them what the big idea is and see if
they really intuitively grasp it, find it interesting, they could share it with others
and they don’t ask for clarification. If you can do that, then you’ve got yourself
a good, big idea. So hopefully, this training is valuable and helpful to you.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
